coredata.dc:title: A target-response ratiometric or turn-off fluorescent dual-mode platform for simultaneous detection of multiple anticancer drugs
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Da-Qian
Zhang, Wenfeng
Yu, Zhendi
Li, Hengye
Fang, Bo
Liu, Guoliang
coredata.dc:description: The anthracycline including doxorubicin (DOX), daunorubicin (DAU) and mitoxantrone (MTX) plays crucial roles in human health due to their notably clinical efficacy in various malignant tumors. There is few molecular probe and abiotic sensor that can simultaneously discriminate among anthracycline drugs. Herein, a target-response ratiometric and turn-off fluorescent dual-mode platform was designed for simultaneous detection of multiple anticancer drugs based on blue-emitting carbon dots (BCDs). In the presence of anthracycline, specific absorption and formation of the BCDs-anthracycline conjugate was achieved via electrostatic interaction and hydrophobic force, leading to ratiometric signal or quenched fluorescent response, thereby achieving ratiometric or turn-off dual-mode detection. Specifically, the introduction of both DOX and DAU produce ratiometric response of BCDs due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and dynamic quenching while MTX only induce reduced fluorescent response attribute to photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and dynamic quenching. The linear range calculated is 1–98, 1–91 and 1–77 μM for DOX, DAU and MTX, respectively, with a limit of detection of 0.02, 0.05 and 0.06 μM. Taking advantage of target-response self-verification ratiometric and sensitive fluorescent detection, the dual-mode platform was proposed and applied for successful discrimination of anthracycline drugs. This study opens a new path for multiplex drugs analysis in a facile and rapid way.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent sensor
Target-response dual mode
Simultaneous detection
Multiple anticancer drugs analysis
Blue-emitting carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Target-response ratiometric mode for DOX and DAU detection based on label-free fluorescent BCDs
Target-response turn-off fluorescent mode for MTX determination based on label-free fluorescent BCDs
Construction of a label-free logic device for the discrimination of anthracyclines

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21501146 and 21974120), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Nos. BK20150424 and BK20140464), and Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Yancheng Institute of Technology (Nos. KYCX24_XZ017 and KYCX23_XY012).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption spectrum (black curve), fluorescence excitation (red curve) and emission spectra (blue curve), (B) HR-TEM image, (C) Fourier transform infrared spectrum (red curve), (D) C1s, (E) O1s, and (F) N1s XPS spectra of prepared BCDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence spectra of synthesized BCDs incubated with different amounts of DOX. (B) The linear correlation between fluorescence ratio values versus DOX concentrations. Conditions: 1, prepared BCDs; 2–16: 1 + DOX (μM): 7, 14, 21, 28, 35, 42, 49, 56, 63, 70, 77, 84, 91, 98, and 105. (C) Fluorescence spectra of synthesized BCDs incubated with different amounts of DAU. (D) The linear correlation between fluorescence ratio values versus DAU concentrations. Conditions: 1, synthesized BCDs; 2–14: 1 + DAU (μM): 7, 14, 21, 28, 35, 42, 49, 56, 63, 70, 77, 84, and 91.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of synthesized BCDs incubated with different amounts of MTX. (B) The linear correlation between fluorescence ratio values versus MTX concentrations. Conditions: 1, synthesized BCDs; 2–19: 1 + MTX (μM): 1, 3.5, 7, 14, 21, 28, 35, 42, 49, 56, 63, 70, 77, 84, 91, 98, 105, and 112. (C) Fluorescence spectra of turn-on detection of ctDNA based on BCDs/MTX system. (D) The linear calibration curve between fluorescence change values and the concentrations of ctDNA. Conditions: 1, synthesized BCDs + 112 μM MTX; 2–15: 1 + ctDNA (μg/mL): 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 65, 80, 95, 110, 125, 140, 155, 170, and 185; pH 8.0.
(A) Different fluorescent characteristics and (B) Image of the CIE1931 chromaticity coordinate calculation of pre-prepared BCDs (1), BCDs + 63 μM DOX (2), BCDs + 63 μM DAU (3), BCDs + 63 μM MTX (4).

The histogram of selectivity assay of the proposed BCDs-based approach for ratiometric (blue histogram) analyzing DOX (63 μM) and DAU (63 μM) or turn-off detecting MTX (63 μM, green histogram) over other possible interfering substances (80 μM). Conditions: 1–21 (from left to right): K+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cl−, NO−, BSA, Pepsin, ATP, Glucose, Ala, Arg, Gly, His, Pro, Leu, Thr, DAU, MTX, and DOX. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Operation of the multiple-inputs logic gate devices based on synthesized BCDs for discriminating DAU from DOX. (A) The histogram of ratiometric value (F568/F440) versus different inputs including input 1 (BSA), input 2 (His), input 3 (ATP), input 4 (Glucose), input 5 (DAU), and input 6 (DOX). The red dashed line shows the threshold (0.60). (B) Truth table for the logic gates with five inputs for the detection of DAU. (C) Truth table for the logic gates with six inputs combinations. Conditions: The concentrations of BSA, histidine, ATP, Glucose, DAU, and DOX are 80 μM, 80 μM, 80 μM, 80 μM, 63 μM and 63 μM, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Schematic illustration of the synthesis of blue-emitting carbon dots and simultaneous discrimination of DOX, DAU, and MTX utilizing ratiometric and turn-off fluorescent dual-mode detection channels. The inserted figures is the relative ratiometric change versus various different inputs (right upper) and the image of the CIE1931 chromaticity coordinate calculation of prepared BCDs without or with anthracyclines (right lower).
Compared our developed approach for detecting DOX or DAU or MTX in human serum samples with the traditional HPLC method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average value of three determinations ± standard deviation.

head.ce:title: A target-response ratiometric or turn-off fluorescent dual-mode platform for simultaneous detection of multiple anticancer drugs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yancheng Tongwei Solar Energy Co., Ltd, Yancheng 224000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Yancheng Tongwei Solar Energy Co., Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yancheng Tongwei Solar Energy Co., Ltd, Yancheng 224000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The anthracycline including doxorubicin (DOX), daunorubicin (DAU) and mitoxantrone (MTX) plays crucial roles in human health due to their notably clinical efficacy in various malignant tumors. There is few molecular probe and abiotic sensor that can simultaneously discriminate among anthracycline drugs. Herein, a target-response ratiometric and turn-off fluorescent dual-mode platform was designed for simultaneous detection of multiple anticancer drugs based on blue-emitting carbon dots (BCDs). In the presence of anthracycline, specific absorption and formation of the BCDs-anthracycline conjugate was achieved via electrostatic interaction and hydrophobic force, leading to ratiometric signal or quenched fluorescent response, thereby achieving ratiometric or turn-off dual-mode detection. Specifically, the introduction of both DOX and DAU produce ratiometric response of BCDs due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and dynamic quenching while MTX only induce reduced fluorescent response attribute to photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and dynamic quenching. The linear range calculated is 1–98, 1–91 and 1–77 μM for DOX, DAU and MTX, respectively, with a limit of detection of 0.02, 0.05 and 0.06 μM. Taking advantage of target-response self-verification ratiometric and sensitive fluorescent detection, the dual-mode platform was proposed and applied for successful discrimination of anthracycline drugs. This study opens a new path for multiplex drugs analysis in a facile and rapid way.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The anthracyclines are a class of widely used anticancer drugs in clinical treatment against various malignant tumors [1,2]. Owing to their high anti-tumor activity property, the anthracyclines includes doxorubicin (DOX), daunorubicin (DAU), and mitoxantrone (MTX) paly major unique roles in human health as broad anti-tumor drug. Accurate monitoring the relative levels of anthracyclines in patient blood possesses an important meaning during cancer treatment for controlling the toxicity. So far, numerous methods including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [3], capillary electrophoresis [4], electrochemistry [5], mass spectrometry [6], chemiluminescence [7], resonance Rayleigh scattering [8], and fluorescence [9] have been reported for selective detection of one or two of anthracyclines. However, the approach that can simultaneously discriminate DOX, DAU, and MTX has little been reported.

Recently, ratiometric fluorescent probe with dual-signal has been developed by using the ratio value between two emission wavelengths to achieve quantitative analysis with effectively eliminating the influence of external factors on the detection results, and thus attracts wide attention among the researchers [10]. To construct a ratiometric sensor, two light-emitting elements need to form a nanoassembly by simply stirring or covalent or non-covalent interactions, undergoing inevitable cumbersome processes [11]. Thus, to develop a new ratiometric fluorescent probe based on a single light-emitting element without conjugated other fluorophores still remains a challenge.

Herein, a target-response ratiometric and turn-off fluorescent dual-mode platform based on sole carbon dots with single emission wavelength was developed for simultaneous discrimination of DOX, DAU, and MTX. In our experimental assays, blue-emitting carbon dots (BCDs) were synthesized by using sodium citrate and bicarbonate amine as the carbon source via one-pot hydrothermal method and selected as the sensor while the anthracyclines were chosen as the analytes. As displayed in Scheme 1 , upon the introduction of three kinds of anthracyclines, two different response modes of intrinsic fluorescence of prepared carbon dots were observed. After adding both DOX and DAU, the synthesized BCDs exhibited a good target-responsive ratiometric property due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and dynamic quenching (Scheme 1, Mode 1). Meanwhile, upon introducing MTX, the prepared BCDs show turn-off fluorescence response attribute to photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and dynamic quenching (Scheme 1, Mode 2). According to different target-response mode, MTX can be firstly discriminated among three kinds of anthracyclines. After that, by utilizing constructed logic device, DAU can be further distinguished from DOX (the histogram, Scheme 1). In addition, the image of the CIE1931 chromaticity coordinate calculation is also performed for observing the differentiation among BCDs without and with DAU, DOX, and MTX in more detail (the image of CIE1931, Scheme 1). This platform paves the new way for multiplex drugs simultaneous analysis in a facile and rapid way.

Sodium citrate and bicarbonate amine (NH4HCO3) were purchased from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Doxorubicin hydrochloride, daunorubicin hydrochloride, mitoxantrone hydrochloride were purchased from Melone Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd (Dalian, China). Calf thymus DNA (ctDNA) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co., Ltd. (USA). All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Millipore ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ. cm) was chosen for all experimental assays. The phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution was chosen for controlling the acidity of the reaction.

The fluorescence spectra were carried out by Jasco FP-6500 fluorescence spectrofluorometer (Jasco, Japan). The absorption spectra were measured by Shimadzu UV-2450 UV–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were performed by a NEXUS-670 FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet, USA). High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images were measured by using a field emission transmission electron microscope at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV (JEM-2100, JEOL Ltd., Japan). The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained by using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Scientific, USA). The pH values were determined by a PHS-25 pH meter (Leici, Shanghai, China).

Blue-emitting fluorescent carbon dots (BCDs) were synthesized according to the reported method via one-step hydrothermal method with minor modification [12]. Briefly, 0.2 g sodium citrate and 1.5 g bicarbonate amine (NH4HCO3) were dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water. After that, the mixture was transferred into Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave, which then put into a drying oven (DHG9070A, Gongyi yuhua instrument Co., Ltd., China) followed by hydrothermal treatment at 180 ℃ for 4 h. Then, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature naturally, and the obtained reaction solution would be removed large precipitates by the centrifugation (12000 rpm for 30 min) for three times. Last, centrifuged BCDs solution was further purified through a dialysis method (1000 Da, molecular weight cut-off) for about 24 h in dark to remove unreacted raw materials.

For the detection of anthracyclines, various concentrations of DOX, DAU, and MTX were added into 2.0 μL synthesized BCDs and then diluted to 3.0 mL with phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 10 mM, pH 8.0) for 1 min.

For the determination of ctDNA, certain concentration of MTX first incubated with prepared BCDs for 3 min. Then, a series of different concentrations of ctDNA were introduced and incubated for 1 min at room temperature. After that, the fluorescence spectra were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm with slit widths at 5 nm for both excitation and emission [13–15].

Fluorescent carbon dots were prepared by using sodium citrate and bicarbonate amine as the carbon source via one-pot hydrothermal method. The characteristic absorption peak of synthesized BCDs was located at 340 nm (Fig. 1 A), which is consist with the reported papers [16]. Meanwhile, upon excitation at 340 nm, the pre-prepared BCDs exhibited quite strong fluorescent signal with a maximum emission peak centered at 435 nm (Fig. 1A). Besides, HR-TEM image was also carried out. As displayed in Fig. 1B, the nanoparticles appeared well-dispersed morphological property with average diameter 2.0 ± 0.5 nm. To further clarify the functional groups of BCDs, the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was also performed (Fig. 1C). The experimental results revealed that 3290, 1650, 1570, 1463, and 1270 cm−1 assigned to secondary amide N–H stretching, secondary C=O stretching, secondary amide C-N stretching, C–H stretching, and C-O stretching. Thus, it was concluded that the functional groups of the synthesized BCDs consisted of hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups [12]. The chemical bonding structures of BCDs were also investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. 1D–F). The C1s spectra can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 284.8 and 288.0 eV, which can be ascribed to C=C/C–C and C-O/C=O bonds, respectively [17]. There is only one peak at 531.4 eV in O1s spectra, assigning to C=O bonds. The N1s spectra can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 399.7 and 399.9 eV, which are associated with C=N bond [18]. These results indicate that there are carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of BCDs which mainly contain carbon, nitrogen, oxygen element. In short, blue-emitting fluorescent CDs (BCDs) were successfully prepared. Moreover, the performed stability experiment reveals that the maximum fluorescent intensity of prepared BCDs nearly kept the content during the testing period (Fig. S1, SI).

As the synthesized BCDs possess abundant carboxyl and amino groups, the effect of pH values on the fluorescent intensity of BCDs were investigated. As displayed in Fig. S2, the fluorescent intensity of BCDs exhibits a mild increase from 4.0 to 8.0, and then remarkably decreases at pH > 8.0 (blue curve). The situation is ascribed to the reversible protonation/de-protonation in acidic and alkaline media [19]. Besides, considering the change of fluorescent intensity of BCDs, BCDs/MTX (green curve) and ratiometric value (F568/F440) of BCDs/DAU (magenta curve) and BCDs/DOX (orange curve), pH 8.0 of PBS buffer was chosen for further experimental assay. In addition, the best fluorescence quenching efficiency or ratiometric change was obtained for 1 min of incubation time (Fig. S3, SI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Target-response ratiometric mode for DOX and DAU detection based on label-free fluorescent BCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The excellent fluorescence properties of prepared BCDs provided a chance for their application in target-responsive analysis. The target-response of synthesized BCDs incubating with anthracyclines were investigated. Interestingly, a ratiometric fluorescence response mode was observed. As displayed in Fig. 2 A, upon the addition of DOX, a new emission peak at 568 nm appeared and increased continuously with increasing DOX concentrations while the emission peak at 440 nm reduced gradually. To further explore the reason for appearing new peak at 568 nm, the fluorescence spectrum of sole DOX without incubated with BCDs was also performed. The experimental results suggested that the maximum emission peak of DOX was located at 560 nm with the excitation at 495 nm (Fig. S4, SI). Thus, the new peak of BCDs after incubated with DOX attributes to the analyte DOX, which provides a new ratiometric design strategy by using synthesized BCDs as the sole fluorophore. Moreover, the DOX concentration was determined through by measuring the ratio of two emissions intensities (F568/F440). The F568/F440 value was directly proportional to the concentration of DOX in the range from 1 to 98 μM with the detection limit of 0.02 μM (S/N = 3) (Fig. 2B). Similarly, the introduction of DAU also produced ratiometric response (Fig. 2C). The dual-emission intensity ratio (F568/F440) increased linearly with the concentration of DAU in the range of 1–91 μM (Fig. 2D). The detection limit of DAU is calculated as 0.05 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Target-response turn-off fluorescent mode for MTX determination based on label-free fluorescent BCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The addition of MTX only induced turn-off fluorescent target-response mode. The experimental results revealed that the fluorescence intensity of BCDs turned up significant decrease upon the introduction of MTX. As shown in Fig. 3 A, as the increasing concentration of MTX, the intensity of maximum emission peak progressively quenched. Besides, the change value of fluorescence intensity (F0-F) was proportional to the concentration of MTX in the range from 1 to 77 μM (Fig. 3B). The detection limit was also calculated to be 0.06 μM.

Moreover, UV–vis absorption titration experiments of prepared BCDs incubated with DOX, DAU and MTX were also carried out. With increasing concentration of DOX, the characteristic peak located at 481 nm appeared and increased gradually (Fig. S5A, SI) while similar conclusion was observed for DAU (Fig. S5B). However, after introducing MTX, a new absorbance peak centered at 610 nm turned up and grew constantly (Fig. S5C). Those experimental results consisted with the conclusion of fluorescent assays with two different target-response mode.

As anthracycline can be intercalated into small groove of double helix of calf thymus DNA (ctDNA) [20], the proposed BCDs/MTX system was further performed for efficiently determine ctDNA. As displayed in Fig. 3C, the quenched fluorescence of BCDs/MTX system emerged remarkable recovery after inducing ctDNA due to competitive binding interaction between MTX and ctDNA, removing MTX from the surface of BCDs and interrupting the electron transfer from BCDs to MTX. Moreover,

the restored fluorescence signal gradually increased after adding the increasing concentration of ctDNA. A good linear calibration plot of the relative fluorescent intensity versus the ctDNA concentration is obtained in the range from 5 to 155 μg/mL (Fig. 3D). The limit of detection is calculated as 0.1 μg/mL.

The mechanism of fluorescence response of BCDs toward anthracyclines has been investigated. After introducing DOX, the maximum emission intensity of as-prepared BCDs located at 435 nm appeared sharp decrease (curve 1, Fig. 4 A) while a new emission peak at 568 nm appeared and increased gradually with increasing DOX concentrations (curve 2). Therefore, the prepared BCDs are applied for constructing a ratiometric sensor for DOX detection without conjugating other fluorophore. Similarly, a ratiometric response of synthesized BCDs for detecting DAU was also observed (curve 3). However, upon the addition of MTX, the emission signal at 435 nm decreased remarkably without a new emission peak at 568 nm (curve 4, Fig. 4A). As possessing hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, anthracyclines adsorbed on the surface of BCDs through by electrostatic attraction and hydrophobic force and resulted in quenched fluorescence of BCDs. Moreover, in order to further distinguish the subtle difference among the emission peaks of synthesized BCDs, BCDs/DOX, BCDs/DAU, and BCDs/MTX system, CIE1931 chromaticity coordinate calculation was implemented (Fig. 4B) [21]. The addition of MTX only induced emission intensity of BCDs (see dots 1 and 4) while the introduction of DOX and DAU led to remarkable red-shift (see dots 2 and 3), revealing different mechanisms of target-response.

Furthermore, to better explore the fluorescence response mechanism of BCDs by anthracyclines, a series of assays were also performed. Firstly, the spectral overlap assay between the fluorescent excitation and emission spectrum of prepared BCDs and the absorption spectrum of analyte was discussed. Fig. S6 revealed that there is an overlap between the absorbance peak of DOX and the emission peak of BCDs in the range of 400–550 nm, suggesting that there is a feasibility of inner filter effect (IFE) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [22,23]. Secondly, the fluorescence lifetime measurements were also determined. As shown in Table S1 (SI), the average fluorescent lifetime appeared an evident decrease after the addition of DOX, suggesting that the fluorescence response of BCDs by anthracyclines is ascribed to FRET rather than IFE and dynamic quenching [24,25]. Similar results are also obtained for DAU. However, the response to MTX by BCDs-based probe is assigned to photoinduced electron transfer (PET) according to the reported work [26,27], which is consistent with different target-response mode. As the fluorescent lifetime appears obvious decrease after the addition of MTX (Table S1), the quenching mechanism of BCDs by MTX is ascribed to PET and dynamic quenching.

To study the selectivity of our developed approach, the response to different foreign substances such as metal ions, inorganic anions, bovine serum albumin (BSA), pepsin, adenosine triphosphate (ATP), glucose, and amino acids was investigated. Upon mixing the interfering substances with the probe, only DAU and DOX could significantly increase the F568/F440 value of synthesized BCDs, whereas other interfered substances have no obvious effect on F568/F440 (the blue histogram, Fig. 5 ). Meanwhile, the interfering substances do not influence on turn-off fluorescent signal for MTX detection (the green histogram). These phenomena indicates that the determination of anthracyclines is not interfered by the tested substances, possessing the promising prospect of quantitative detection of anthracycline drugs in clinical samples. In addition, tested interfering substances including metal ions, anions,
amino acids, glucose, and BSA do not nearly influence on the detection of ctDNA based on BCDs/MTX-based sensing system (Fig. S7, SI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of a label-free logic device for the discrimination of anthracyclines
ce:section.ce:para: To further discriminate DOX and DAU, a label-free logic gate with the analytes and interfering substances as multiple inputs has been constructed [28–30]. The BCDs displayed strong fluorescent signal at 440 nm. After that, upon the addition of analyte DOX and DAU, it appears the new emission peak located at 568 nm. Meanwhile, the introduction of interfering substances does not produce the ratiometic signal. Moreover, the ratio value (F568/F440) served as the output (“1″ or “0”) with a threshold value of 0.60 (Fig. 6 A). The basic sensing mechanism and judgment rules based on combinatorial logic gates are summarized in Fig. 6B and C.

First, the sample a1 is incubated with BCDs (input 00000, No. a1, the first line in Fig. 6B). The result reveals that it does not turn up new emission peak at 568 nm (output “0″). After that, BSA (input 1) is mixed with sample a1/BCDs (input 10000, NO. a2, the second line), only output “0” is observed. However, upon the addition of the analyte DAU (input 5) to sample a2 (input 10001, NO. a3, the third line), it appears new emission peak at 568 nm with the fluorescent intensity above the dashed line (output “1”). Similarly, mono-inputs including input 2 (His, input 01000, NO. a4, the fourth line) or input 3 (ATP, input 00100, NO. a6, the sixth line) or input 4 (Glucose, input 00010, NO. a8, the eighth line) results in output “0” while two inputs including input 2 and the analyte (DAU, input 5) (input 01001, NO. a5, the fifth line), input 00,101 (NO. a7, the seventh line), input 00,011 (NO. a9, the ninth line) produces output “1”.

Second, without input 5, bi-inputs combinations including inputs 11,000 (NO. a10, the tenth line), 01,100 (NO. a11, the eleventh line), 00,110 (NO. a12, the twelfth line), and 01,010 (NO. a13, the thirteenth line) induce output “0″ while tri-inputs combinations consisting of inputs 11,100 (NO. a14, the fourteenth line), 10,110 (NO. a15, the fifteenth line), 11,010 (NO. a16, the sixteenth line), and 01,110 (NO. a17, the seventeenth line) result in output “0”. Meanwhile, tetra-inputs combination, for example, input 11,110 (NO. a18, the eighteenth line) only produces output “0”. However, upon the introduction of input 5 (penta-inputs combination, input 11111, NO. a19, the nineteenth line), it appears remarkable ratiometric signal with the intensity above the dashed line, named as output “1”. Thus, combined with the results above, both input 1, input 2, input 3, and input 4 do not interfere determination of DAU by using our proposed approach.

Third, to further discriminate two kinds of anthracycline anticancer drugs (DAU and DOX), the response of the developed approach to DOX was also investigated and the experimental results were shown in Fig. 6C. Six inputs including BSA (input 1), His (input 2), ATP (input 3), Glucose (input 4), DAU (input 5), and DOX (input 6) were designed and chosen as multi-inputs for constructing the logic gate. When BSA, His, ATP, and Glucose (tetra-inputs) (input 111100, NO. b1, the first line in Fig. 6C), it does not turn up significant enhancement at 568 nm, called as output “0″. After that, the addition of DAU (the analyte) into sample b1 (input 111110, NO. b2, the second line), output “1” is obtained. However, upon the introduction of input 6 (DOX) into sample b1 (input 111101, NO. b3, the third line) produces certain ratiometric signal change but that is below the threshold value, still served as the output “0”. Mono-input sample b4 (input 000010, the fourth line) results in output “1” while sample b5 (input 000001, the fifth line) only induces output “0”. Besides, both input 5 and input 6 simultaneously are incubated with the probe (input 000011, NO. b6, the sixth line), output “1” is observed. In addition, for hexa-inputs design, through by the addition of input 5 into sample b3 (input 111111, NO. b7, the seventh line), output “1” is determined. Therefore, via constructing the label-free molecular logic gates, DAU can be further discriminated from DOX.

To further assess the feasibility of proposed dual target-response platform for real sample determination, BCDs-based fluorescent platform is performed to detection anthracyclines in human serum samples. Healthy serum sample was collected and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min. After that, the supernatant fluid was applied for further determination. The detection of DOX, DAU and MTX in real serum samples was performed by using the standard addition method. Meanwhile, traditional High Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) method was executed as the control and the results are shown in Table 1 . Good consistency for the amounts of detecting anthracyclines obtained by two methods was observed, revealing that our developed platform possessed promising application prospect of monitoring anticancer drugs in clinical samples. Moreover, compared with reported methods, our proposed approach exhibits comparable sensitivity with wider linear ranges (Table S2) [31–37].

In summary, a label-free target-response ratiometric and turn-off fluorescent dual-mode platform for simultaneous detection of multiple anticancer drugs was first developed based on sole blue-emitting carbon dots. The present approach addresses the issues of traditional ratiometric sensors such as dual chromophores linking or nanocomposites synthesis. Through using DOX or DAU as both the fluorophore and the analyte, the pre-prepared BCDs showed an excellent target-responsive ratiometric property without conjugated other fluorogen attribute to ICT and dynamic quenching. Meanwhile, MTX only induced turn-off fluorescent signal of synthesized BCDs without the appearance of new emission peak due to PET and dynamic quenching. Based on these phenomena, the present probe design strategy provides a new path for concurrently distinguish DOX, DAU and MTX.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21501146 and 21974120), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Nos. BK20150424 and BK20140464), and Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Yancheng Institute of Technology (Nos. KYCX24_XZ017 and KYCX23_XY012).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.126173.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
